:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Each crime in Louisiana appears to have a statutory maximum. For example, theft of something with a value of $25,000 or over will result in imprisonment of not more than twenty years or a fine of not more than fifty thousand dollars, or both. Any sentence to imprisonment must be “determinate,” in other words, judges sentence individuals to a maximum sentence rather than a range with minimum and maximum limits. Eligibility for parole is determined by type of crime and by criminal history. Unusually, prisoners who have been convicted of non-violent, non-sexual offenses can also age out of the Louisiana prison system even if they are ineligible for parole. If sentenced to a term of over 30 years, a prisoner who has served at least 20 of those years and has reached the age of 45 is eligible for parole consideration. If sentenced to any term without the benefit of parole, a prisoner who has reached the age of 60 and has completed various institutional requirements may be eligible for parole.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 2,218 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 8% of the prison population.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Unknown people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 100% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 94% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 34% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 10-24.9 years, accounting for 42% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to drugs, accounting for 27% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::
